passages.text: The Byzantine Empire possessed a formidable secret weapon known as Greek fire, an incendiary substance that played a crucial role in its military history. ( ① ) The exact formula for this chemical weapon was a closely guarded state secret, and its composition remains a subject of scholarly debate. ( ② ) What is known is that it could be projected from siphons mounted on ships and would continue to burn even on water. ( ③ ) This made it an exceptionally effective naval weapon, giving the Byzantine fleet a significant advantage over its enemies. ( ④ ) The terrifying nature of the weapon also had a profound psychological impact on opposing forces, who often broke rank and fled at its sight. ( ⑤ ) The loss of the formula for Greek fire sometime after the 12th century marked a significant decline in the military power of the empire.

passages.text: Social mobility, the movement of individuals or groups in social position over time, is a key concept in sociology. It can be measured in terms of changes in income, occupation, or social class. ( ① ) While societies vary in their degree of openness, most modern nations provide pathways for upward mobility. ( ② ) Several factors are known to influence an individual's chances of moving up the social ladder, including family background and economic conditions. ( ③ ) However, among the most powerful engines of social mobility is education. ( ④ ) This is because education equips individuals with the necessary skills, knowledge, and credentials to compete for better-paying and more prestigious jobs. ( ⑤ ) Therefore, policies aimed at improving educational equity are often seen as a primary tool for fostering a more mobile and just society.

passages.text: The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis, also known as linguistic relativity, posits that the structure of a language affects its speakers' worldview or cognition. ( ① ) This theory suggests that the language we speak not only voices our thoughts but also shapes them. ( ② ) There are two versions of the hypothesis: a "strong" version, which claims language determines thought, and a "weak" version, which argues that language merely influences it. ( ③ ) Most modern linguists support the weaker version, acknowledging that language can guide and predispose us to certain ways of thinking. ( ④ ) For instance, the Pirahã people of the Amazon rainforest have a language that lacks specific words for numbers, instead using general terms like "few" and "many." ( ⑤ ) Such examples suggest a compelling link between the words we use and how we interpret the world around us.

passages.text: Cubism, one of the most influential art movements of the early 20th century, represented a radical departure from traditional European painting. Pioneered by Pablo Picasso and Georges Braque, it challenged the long-held convention that art should imitate nature. ( ① ) Instead of depicting objects from a single, fixed viewpoint, Cubist painters portrayed their subjects from a multitude of viewpoints simultaneously. ( ② ) This technique resulted in fragmented, geometric forms that presented a more complex and conceptual reality. ( ③ ) The movement sought to represent the essence of an object by capturing it in its entirety, over time, within a single canvas. ( ④ ) Picasso's 1907 painting *Les Demoiselles d'Avignon*, for example, is considered a seminal work that shattered traditional notions of perspective and form. ( ⑤ ) This revolutionary approach paved the way for many subsequent abstract art movements.

passages.text: The philosopher John Locke, a key figure in empiricism, proposed the concept of *tabula rasa*, or "blank slate." According to this theory, the human mind is born without any innate ideas or knowledge. ( ① ) Instead, all knowledge is gradually acquired through sensory experience and reflection. ( ② ) Locke argued that we gather simple ideas from our senses—such as "yellow," "cold," or "soft"—and then combine them in our minds to form more complex ideas, like "a cold, yellow lemon." ( ③ ) This perspective directly challenged the rationalist view, championed by philosophers like Descartes, that certain principles are inherent in the human mind from birth. ( ④ ) To illustrate, a child who is bitten by a dog develops a fear of dogs not from an innate idea of fear, but from the direct negative experience. ( ⑤ ) Thus, for Locke, experience is the ultimate source of all human knowledge and character.

passages.text: In evolutionary biology, Batesian mimicry is a form of defensive adaptation in which a harmless species evolves to imitate the warning signals of a harmful species. The purpose of this mimicry is to deceive predators into believing that the mimic is also dangerous or unpalatable. ( ① ) This strategy is only successful if the predator has had a negative encounter with the genuinely harmful species, known as the model. ( ② ) Once the predator learns to avoid the model, it will also tend to avoid the mimic. ( ③ ) For instance, the harmless viceroy butterfly has evolved to mimic the distinct orange and black wing patterns of the toxic monarch butterfly. ( ④ ) The effectiveness of this mimicry depends on the population ratio; the mimic must be less common than the model for the predator's learned avoidance to remain strong. ( ⑤ ) If mimics become too numerous, predators may learn that many of the warning-colored insects are actually safe to eat.

passages.text: A fundamental principle in economics is the concept of opportunity cost, which is the value of the next-best alternative that must be forgone to pursue a certain action. It is a key aspect of decision-making because resources like time, money, and effort are always limited. ( ① ) Every choice we make involves a trade-off. ( ② ) The opportunity cost is not just the monetary price of a choice but includes all the benefits you miss out on by not choosing the alternative. ( ③ ) To illustrate, if you spend three hours and $50 attending a concert, you cannot spend that time studying for an exam or that money on a new book. ( ④ ) Understanding this concept helps individuals and businesses make more rational and efficient choices by explicitly considering what they are giving up. ( ⑤ ) It forces a clear-eyed evaluation of what is truly most valuable in any given situation.

passages.text: Cognitive dissonance is a psychological theory that refers to the mental stress or discomfort experienced by an individual who holds two or more contradictory beliefs, ideas, or values, or when their beliefs are inconsistent with their actions. ( ① ) This theory, proposed by Leon Festinger, suggests that people have a motivational drive to reduce this dissonance by changing their attitudes, beliefs, or behaviors. ( ② ) The discomfort is often so strong that it compels individuals to seek consistency. ( ③ ) For example, a person who considers themselves environmentally conscious but drives a gas-guzzling SUV experiences this form of mental discomfort. ( ④ ) These resolutions are all attempts to restore balance and reduce the psychological tension. ( ⑤ ) Understanding cognitive dissonance provides insight into why people sometimes make seemingly irrational decisions or change their minds unexpectedly.

passages.text: A trophic cascade is an ecological phenomenon triggered by the addition or removal of top predators, which results in dramatic changes in the ecosystem structure and nutrient cycling. These powerful indirect interactions can control entire ecosystems. ( ① ) When a top predator is removed, the population of its primary prey can explode. ( ② ) This, in turn, can lead to the overconsumption of primary producers like plants, fundamentally altering the landscape. ( ③ ) For instance, the reintroduction of wolves into Yellowstone National Park in 1995 triggered a cascade of ecological changes. ( ④ ) The wolves preyed on elk, causing a decrease in the elk population and changing their grazing behavior. ( ⑤ ) This example powerfully demonstrates how a single keystone species can shape the health and structure of its entire environment.

( ③ ) The increased absorption of solar radiation leads to further warming of the ocean, which in turn causes even more sea ice to melt. ( ④ ) This cycle creates a self-reinforcing process that significantly accelerates the rate of warming in the Arctic region, with global consequences. ( ⑤ ) Understanding these mechanisms is crucial for accurately predicting future climate scenarios.

passages.text: The Earth's climate system is characterized by complex feedback loops, where an initial change can trigger a series of subsequent changes that either amplify or dampen the original effect. One of the most critical examples of an amplifying feedback loop involves the melting of Arctic sea ice. ( ① ) As global temperatures rise, the white, reflective sea ice begins to melt, exposing the darker ocean water beneath it. ( ② ) This darker surface has a lower albedo, meaning it absorbs more of the sun's energy rather than reflecting it back into space. ( ③ ) The increased absorption of solar radiation leads to further warming of the ocean, which in turn causes even more sea ice to melt. ( ④ ) This phenomenon, known as the ice-albedo feedback, is a powerful amplifier of climate change. ( ⑤ ) Understanding these mechanisms is crucial for accurately predicting future climate scenarios.

passages.text: In macroeconomics, the relationship between inflation and unemployment has long been a subject of debate. The traditional view, first observed by A.W. ( ② ) This heightened demand would, in turn, lead to higher wages and prices, resulting in inflation. ( ③ ) Conversely, measures aimed at curbing inflation, like raising interest rates, would likely slow down economic activity and lead to higher unemployment. ( ④ ) This perspective dominated economic policy for decades, suggesting that a government could not simultaneously achieve both low inflation and low unemployment. ( ⑤ ) However, later economic events, such as the stagflation of the 1970s, challenged this simple trade-off.

passages.text: The theory of cognitive dissonance, proposed by Leon Festinger, explores the mental discomfort experienced by a person who holds two or more contradictory beliefs, ideas, or values, or is confronted by new information that conflicts with existing beliefs. ( ① ) For instance, a person who considers themselves environmentally conscious might purchase a gas-guzzling SUV. ( ② ) This conflict between their belief ("I am environmentally friendly") and their action (buying an inefficient car) creates a sense of internal inconsistency. ( ③ ) Such a state of psychological discomfort is what Festinger termed "cognitive dissonance." ( ④ ) They might sell the car, but more often, they will change their attitude or belief. For example, they might start to believe that one person's car choice has a negligible impact on the environment or that the SUV's safety features are more important. ( ⑤ ) This change reduces the mental discomfort and restores a sense of balance.

passages.text: In the legal system, the rules of evidence are designed to ensure that trials are fair and verdicts are based on reliable information. One of the most fundamental of these is the hearsay rule, which generally prohibits the use of out-of-court statements to prove the truth of the matter asserted. ( ① ) The primary reason for this rule is that the person who originally made the statement (the declarant) is not in court, under oath, and subject to cross-examination. ( ② ) This lack of opportunity for scrutiny makes such statements inherently less reliable than direct testimony. ( ③ ) However, the law recognizes numerous exceptions to this rule where the hearsay statement is deemed to have a high degree of trustworthiness. ( ④ ) This is known as the "excited utterance" exception. ( ⑤ ) Without these carefully crafted rules, the integrity of the judicial process could be compromised.

passages.text: The principle of the separation of powers is a cornerstone of modern democratic governance, most famously articulated by Montesquieu. ( ① ) Typically, these are the legislative branch (which makes laws), the executive branch (which enforces laws), and the judicial branch (which interprets laws). ( ② ) The core idea is to prevent the concentration of power in any single entity, which could lead to tyranny or abuse. ( ③ ) This division of authority is not absolute; instead, it is structured to create a system of checks and balances. ( ④ ) In turn, the legislature can override that veto, and the judiciary can declare the law unconstitutional. ( ⑤ ) This intricate system ensures that power is shared and that each branch can hold the others accountable.

passages.text: Gentrification is a complex urban process whereby a neighborhood undergoes a significant transformation, often marked by an influx of wealthier residents and investment. ( ② ) Their presence often makes the area more fashionable and appealing. ( ③ ) This demographic shift attracts real estate developers and new businesses, who see potential for profit, further accelerating the rise in housing costs. ( ④ ) This displacement is one of the most controversial aspects of the process. ( ⑤ ) While gentrification can lead to reduced crime rates and improved infrastructure, it also raises critical questions about social equity and the right to the city.

passages.text: Ecosystems are intricate webs of relationships, and the removal of a single species can have unexpected and far-reaching consequences. A classic example of this is the concept of a trophic cascade, which illustrates how predators at the top of the food chain can indirectly influence the entire ecosystem structure. ( ① ) Consider the reintroduction of wolves to Yellowstone National Park. ( ② ) Before their return, the elk population had grown unchecked, leading to overgrazing of willow and aspen trees, especially along rivers. ( ③ ) When wolves were reintroduced, they began to prey on the elk, causing the elk to not only decrease in number but also change their behavior to avoid certain areas. ( ④ ) This led to the recovery of riverside vegetation, which in turn stabilized riverbanks, provided habitats for birds and beavers, and even changed the course of the rivers themselves. ( ⑤ ) This cascading effect, starting from the apex predator and flowing down through the food web, demonstrates the profound impact of keystone species.

passages.text: The relationship between language and thought has been a central question in linguistics and psychology, most famously encapsulated in the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis. ( ① ) This hypothesis posits that the structure of a language affects its speakers' worldview or cognition. ( ② ) The "strong" version, known as linguistic determinism, argues that language entirely determines our thought processes—we can only think in the categories provided by our language. ( ③ ) This deterministic view is now largely rejected by the scientific community as it is too rigid and lacks empirical support. ( ④ ) Instead, a "weak" version of the hypothesis, called linguistic relativity, has gained more acceptance. ( ⑤ ) For example, a language with many words for different types of snow may encourage its speakers to pay closer attention to those distinctions, but it does not prevent speakers of other languages from perceiving them.

passages.text: Utilitarianism, a prominent ethical theory developed by philosophers like Jeremy Bentham and John Stuart Mill, proposes that the best moral action is the one that maximizes utility, usually defined as that which maximizes happiness or well-being for the greatest number of people. ( ① ) This principle, often summarized as "the greatest good for the greatest number," provides a straightforward framework for making ethical decisions. ( ② ) For example, when deciding on a public policy, a utilitarian would weigh the total benefits it brings to society against the total costs and harms. ( ③ ) If the overall happiness increases, the policy is considered morally right. ( ④ ) Such a calculation, however, raises a significant ethical problem. ( ⑤ ) This challenge highlights the tension between utilitarian calculations and the concept of inalienable individual rights.

passages.text: Vaccines are a cornerstone of modern medicine, working by preparing the body's immune system to fight off future infections. The process begins with the introduction of a weakened or inactivated form of a pathogen, such as a virus or bacterium, into the body. ( ① ) This harmless version of the pathogen is known as an antigen. ( ② ) Upon detecting this antigen, the immune system is triggered to mount a response, just as it would against a real infection. ( ③ ) It produces antibodies, which are specialized proteins that can neutralize the invader. ( ④ ) These cells remain in the body for a long time, sometimes for a lifetime. ( ⑤ ) If the person is later exposed to the actual, harmful pathogen, these memory cells can quickly recognize it and launch a rapid and powerful immune response, preventing the disease from developing.

passages.text: Bioremediation is an innovative environmental technology that uses living organisms, primarily microorganisms, to clean up contaminated sites. ( ② ) These microbes essentially "eat" the pollutants, using them as a source of food and energy. ( ③ ) This transformation is the core of the cleanup process, effectively neutralizing the threat to the ecosystem. ( ④ ) The effectiveness of bioremediation depends on various factors, including temperature, nutrient availability, and the type of contaminant. ( ⑤ ) It is considered a green solution because it relies on natural processes and minimizes the need for harsh chemicals.

passages.text: Blockchain technology is most famous for powering cryptocurrencies, but its core principles have far-reaching implications. The defining feature of blockchain is its decentralized nature. ( ① ) Unlike traditional databases that are stored in a central location, a blockchain is a distributed ledger, meaning an identical copy of the transaction record is held by numerous computers across a network. ( ② ) Whenever a new transaction occurs, it is verified by the network participants and added as a new "block" to the "chain." ( ③ ) As a result, no single entity has control over the entire network, which enhances security and transparency. ( ④ ) If a malicious actor wanted to alter a transaction, they would need to alter the block on a majority of the computers in the network simultaneously, which is practically impossible. ( ⑤ ) This characteristic makes blockchain a promising technology for applications beyond finance, including supply chain management and voting systems.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.

passages.text: Rituals are a fundamental aspect of human culture, found in every known society. They are structured sequences of actions and words that are performed in a specific order. ( ① ) While the content of rituals varies widely—from religious ceremonies to national holidays—their social function often remains consistent. ( ② ) One of the primary functions is to bring people together and create a sense of community. ( ③ ) During a ritual, individuals set aside their personal concerns and focus on a common purpose or belief, acting in a synchronized manner. ( ④ ) Consequently, these shared experiences strengthen the bonds among participants and reinforce a sense of collective identity. ( ⑤ ) Therefore, rituals are not just empty traditions; they are powerful tools for building and sustaining social groups.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.

passages.text: The educational psychologist Lev Vygotsky introduced the concept of the Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD) to describe the sweet spot for learning. The ZPD is the gap between what a learner can do independently and what they can achieve with guidance from a more knowledgeable person. ( ① ) This guide could be a teacher, a peer, or even a parent. ( ② ) Vygotsky's theory posits that learning is most effective when it occurs within this zone. ( ③ ) For instance, a child might not be able to solve a complex math problem alone, but they can solve it with targeted hints from a teacher. ( ④ ) As a result, the learner can successfully perform the task and internalize the new skill, effectively expanding their independent capabilities. ( ⑤ ) This process is not about simply being given the answer, but about being guided to discover it.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.

passages.text: Photosynthesis is the vital process by which plants, algae, and some bacteria convert light energy into chemical energy. This complex process can be divided into two main stages. ( ① ) The first stage is known as the light-dependent reactions, which take place in the thylakoid membranes of chloroplasts. ( ② ) During this stage, chlorophyll and other pigments absorb energy from sunlight. ( ③ ) This captured light energy is used to split water molecules, releasing oxygen as a byproduct. ( ④ ) Consequently, the energy captured from sunlight is stored in the chemical bonds of molecules like ATP and NADPH. ( ⑤ ) In the Calvin cycle, this stored chemical energy is used to convert carbon dioxide from the atmosphere into glucose, a sugar that provides energy for the plant's growth and development.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.

passages.text: The Earth's crust is in a constant state of change, leading to the formation of three main types of rocks: igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic. Metamorphic rocks are formed from the transformation of existing rock types in a process called metamorphism. ( ① ) This process occurs when pre-existing rocks are subjected to intense heat, high pressure, or chemical reactions deep within the Earth. ( ② ) The conditions for metamorphism are typically found where tectonic plates collide or where magma intrudes into the surrounding rock. ( ③ ) As a result, the original rock's mineral composition and texture are fundamentally altered, creating a new type of rock. ( ④ ) The degree of change depends on the intensity of the heat and pressure applied. ( ⑤ ) This transformation does not melt the rock, but rather recrystallizes it, distinguishing metamorphic rocks from igneous rocks, which form from molten material.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 변성 작용의 구체적인 예시(석회암→대리석)를 제공합니다. (③)에서 변성 작용의 결과로 새로운 암석이 생성된다는 일반적인 설명을 한 후, 그에 대한 예시로 (④)에 들어가는 것이 가장 자연스럽습니다.

passages.text: The law of supply and demand is a fundamental concept in economics that explains how prices are determined in a market economy. It states that the price of a good will adjust to the point where the quantity demanded by consumers equals the quantity supplied by producers. ( ① ) This point is known as the equilibrium price. ( ② ) However, markets are not always in equilibrium. ( ③ ) When the quantity supplied of a good exceeds the quantity demanded at a certain price, a situation known as a surplus occurs. ( ④ ) Consequently, producers are often forced to lower their prices to sell off the excess inventory and attract more buyers. ( ⑤ ) Conversely, if demand exceeds supply, a shortage occurs, which tends to drive prices up.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 (④)에서 시작된 가격 인하가 언제까지 계속되는지를 설명합니다. 따라서 생산자들이 가격을 낮춘다는 내용 바로 뒤인 (⑤)에 와서 그 과정의 지속과 끝을 설명하는 것이 가장 논리적입니다.

passages.text: Classical conditioning is a learning process first demonstrated by the Russian physiologist Ivan Pavlov. His famous experiments with dogs revealed how associations between stimuli can lead to new behaviors. ( ① ) In his setup, the presentation of food (an unconditioned stimulus) naturally caused the dogs to salivate (an unconditioned response). ( ② ) Pavlov then began to ring a bell (a neutral stimulus) just before presenting the food. ( ③ ) He repeated this pairing of the bell and the food multiple times. ( ④ ) As a result, the neutral stimulus alone begins to elicit the response that was originally triggered only by the unconditioned stimulus. ( ⑤ ) This principle explains how many involuntary responses, such as fears and phobias, can be learned.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 (④)에서 설명된 현상, 즉 중성 자극만으로 반응이 유발되는 그 시점에서 일어나는 개념적 변화(중성 자극→조건 자극)를 정의합니다. 따라서 현상 설명 뒤, 그리고 전체 원리 요약 앞에 위치해야 합니다.

passages.text: Osmosis is a crucial biological process that involves the movement of water across a semipermeable membrane. This type of membrane allows small molecules like water to pass through but blocks larger molecules like salt or sugar (solutes). ( ① ) The direction of water movement is determined by the concentration of solutes on either side of the membrane. ( ② ) Specifically, water will always move from an area of lower solute concentration to an area of higher solute concentration. ( ③ ) This net movement of water continues until the concentrations reach a state of balance or equilibrium. ( ④ ) Consequently, the concentration of the solute becomes more equal on both sides of the membrane. ( ⑤ ) Without osmosis, cells would not be able to maintain their internal environment and would quickly perish.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 삼투 현상이라는 '원리(principle)'가 생물학적으로 왜 중요한지를 구체적인 예시를 들어 설명합니다. (④)까지 삼투 현상의 과정과 결과가 모두 설명되었으므로, 그 원리의 중요성을 설명하는 주어진 문장이 (⑤)에 오는 것이 가장 적절합니다.

passages.text: The formation of the Himalayas is a dramatic story of geological change, a direct result of plate tectonics. Millions of years ago, the landmass that is now the Indian subcontinent was an island moving steadily northward across the Tethys Ocean. ( ① ) This journey culminated in a colossal collision with the Eurasian Plate. ( ② ) The immense pressure from this impact had to be released. ( ③ ) In addition, because both were continental plates of similar density, neither could be subducted under the other. ( ④ ) This process is not over; the Indian Plate continues to push against the Eurasian Plate, causing the Himalayas to rise by a few millimeters each year. ( ⑤ ) This ongoing tectonic activity is also the reason for the region's frequent and powerful earthquakes.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 '대신에(Instead)'라는 표현을 사용하여, (③)에서 설명된 '섭입 불가' 상황의 결과를 설명합니다. 즉, 아래로 가지 못하고 위로 솟아올라 산맥을 형성했다는 내용이므로 (④)가 가장 적절합니다.

passages.text: The mid-19th century witnessed a radical transformation in naval warfare, primarily driven by technological advancements. For centuries, wooden sailing ships had dominated the seas, but their vulnerability was starkly exposed by the invention of explosive naval shells. ( ① ) These shells could easily tear through wooden hulls, rendering traditional warships obsolete. ( ② ) In response, naval engineers began cladding ships in thick iron plates, creating the first "ironclads." ( ③ ) Furthermore, the adoption of steam engines freed these new warships from the unpredictability of the wind, granting them unprecedented tactical mobility. ( ④ ) The Battle of Hampton Roads during the American Civil War famously demonstrated the superiority of ironclads over their wooden counterparts, marking a definitive end to the age of sail in military contexts. ( ⑤ ) This combination of armor and independent propulsion defined the next generation of naval power.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 철갑선이 적의 포화에 더 강했다는 장점을 설명합니다. (②)에서 철갑선이 처음 만들어졌다고 소개한 후, 그 장점을 설명하는 (③)에 들어가는 것이 가장 자연스럽습니다. 그 뒤에 또 다른 장점(증기 엔진)이 추가됩니다.

passages.text: For nearly three decades after World War II, the global economy operated under the Bretton Woods system, which pegged major currencies to the U.S. dollar, itself convertible to gold at a fixed rate. This created a stable, predictable environment for international trade. ( ① ) However, by the early 1970s, economic pressures, particularly from U.S. spending on the Vietnam War and domestic programs, made the system unsustainable. ( ② ) In 1971, the United States unilaterally terminated the convertibility of the dollar to gold, effectively collapsing the Bretton Woods agreement. ( ③ ) This led to the modern era of floating exchange rates, where currency values are determined by supply and demand in the foreign exchange market. ( ④ ) In addition, this new system allowed countries to use monetary policy more freely to address domestic economic issues like unemployment or inflation. ( ⑤ )

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 변동 환율제의 단점(변동성, 불확실성)을 설명합니다. (③)에서 변동 환율제가 도입되었다고 설명한 후, 그 특징 중 하나로 (④)에 들어가는 것이 가장 논리적입니다.

passages.text: The transition from medieval art to the Renaissance marked one of the most significant shifts in Western cultural history. Medieval art was predominantly religious in theme and symbolic in nature, often depicting figures in a flat, stylized manner without a strong sense of realism. ( ① ) The primary goal was to convey spiritual truths rather than to represent the natural world accurately. ( ② ) With the dawn of the Renaissance, however, a renewed interest in classical humanism led to a profound change in artistic focus. ( ③ ) Artists started to celebrate the human form, studying anatomy to portray figures with lifelike accuracy and emotional depth. ( ④ ) Furthermore, artists began to employ new techniques like linear perspective to create a convincing illusion of three-dimensional space on a two-dimensional surface. ( ⑤ ) This combination of humanistic subjects and realistic techniques defined the art of the era.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 사실주의의 추구가 '세계 자체의 묘사'로 확장되었음을 말합니다. (③)에서 인물 묘사의 사실주의를 설명한 후, (④)에 들어가 주제를 확장하고, 그 뒤에 구체적인 기법(선형 원근법)을 설명하는 것이 자연스럽습니다.

passages.text: The proliferation of social network services (SNS) has fundamentally altered the dynamics of information diffusion. In traditional media, information flowed in a top-down manner, from a few sources to a large audience. ( ① ) On SNS, however, any user can be a source, leading to a more decentralized and complex pattern of dissemination. ( ② ) The virality of a piece of content often depends on its perceived novelty, emotional resonance, and the credibility of the initial sharer. ( ③ ) A message originating from a highly trusted individual is more likely to be accepted and passed on. ( ④ ) In addition, the network structure itself—who is connected to whom—plays a critical role in how widely and quickly information spreads. ( ⑤ ) Understanding these factors is crucial for anyone trying to communicate effectively in the digital age.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 '네트워크 구조'의 한계(메아리 방)에 대한 구체적인 예시입니다. (④)에서 네트워크 구조의 중요성을 언급한 후, 그 구체적인 예시로 (⑤)에 들어가는 것이 가장 적절합니다.

passages.text: The Crusades, a series of religious wars between the 11th and 13th centuries, are often remembered for their military conflicts. However, their long-term impact on European society extended far beyond the battlefield. ( ① ) One of the most significant consequences was the expansion of trade between Europe and the Middle East. ( ② ) Italian city-states like Venice and Genoa grew wealthy by transporting crusaders and establishing new trade routes for spices, silks, and other luxury goods. ( ③ ) This commercial revival helped to weaken the feudal system, as a new merchant class emerged and the economy shifted from being land-based to more reliant on cash. ( ④ ) Furthermore, the returning Crusaders brought back new goods, scientific knowledge, and philosophical ideas from the more advanced Islamic world, stimulating a cultural and intellectual awakening in Europe. ( ⑤ ) Thus, the Crusades inadvertently helped to lay the groundwork for modern Europe.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 (④)에서 언급된 '지식의 유입'이 르네상스의 지적 발효에 기여했다는 결과를 설명합니다. 따라서 지식 유입에 대한 설명 바로 뒤인 (⑤)에 위치해야 합니다.

passages.text: The evolution from Impressionism to Post-Impressionism is clearly visible in the career of Vincent van Gogh. Initially, his work was influenced by the Impressionists' focus on capturing the fleeting effects of light and atmosphere. ( ① ) Like them, he painted outdoors and depicted everyday scenes. ( ② ) However, van Gogh soon grew dissatisfied with what he saw as the Impressionists' superficial concern for visual reality. ( ③ ) He sought to imbue his art with deeper personal meaning and psychological intensity. ( ④ ) In addition, he began to use thick, visible brushstrokes and vivid, often unnatural colors to convey his emotional response to a subject, rather than its objective appearance. ( ⑤ ) His famous work, "The Starry Night," for example, is less a depiction of a night sky and more an expression of his turbulent inner world.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 반 고흐의 새로운 화풍이 인상주의의 '객관적 관찰' 목표에서 벗어났음을 요약합니다. (④)에서 구체적인 기법을 설명한 후, 그 기법의 의의를 설명하는 (⑤)에 들어가는 것이 가장 적절합니다.

passages.text: The theory of plate tectonics, developed in the mid-20th century, revolutionized our understanding of the Earth. It built upon the earlier idea of continental drift, proposed by Alfred Wegener in 1912. ( ① ) Wegener had amassed considerable evidence—such as the jigsaw-puzzle fit of continents and fossil similarities across oceans—but he could not explain the force that moved the continents. ( ② ) His theory was therefore largely dismissed by the scientific community for decades. ( ③ ) Plate tectonics filled this crucial gap by proposing that the Earth's outer shell is divided into several large plates that move over the molten upper layer of the mantle. ( ④ ) Furthermore, the theory provided a unifying mechanism for phenomena that had previously seemed unrelated, such as earthquakes, volcanic activity, and the formation of mountain ranges. ( ⑤ ) This comprehensive explanatory power is why plate tectonics is now considered the cornerstone of modern geology.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 판 구조론이 대륙 이동의 '원동력'을 어떻게 설명했는지를 구체적으로 밝힙니다. (③)에서 판 구조론이 그 공백을 메웠다고 한 후, 그 구체적인 설명으로 (④)에 들어가는 것이 가장 자연스럽습니다.

passages.text: The fluctuation of a country's currency exchange rate has profound effects on its international trade and domestic economy. When a country's currency appreciates, or strengthens, against foreign currencies, it means that one unit of the domestic currency can buy more foreign currency. ( ① ) This makes imported goods cheaper for domestic consumers, which can help to control inflation. ( ② ) However, this same strength can be a double-edged sword. ( ③ ) For foreign buyers, a stronger domestic currency means that the country's export products become more expensive, which can lead to a decrease in export volume. ( ④ ) Conversely, when a currency depreciates, or weakens, imports become more expensive for domestic consumers. ( ⑤ ) In addition, a weaker domestic currency makes a country's exports cheaper and more competitive on the global market, potentially boosting sales and production.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 통화 가치 변동이 가져오는 복잡한 장단점을 요약하는 결론 문장입니다. 통화 강세와 약세의 장단점을 모두 설명한 후인 (⑤)에 위치하여 글 전체를 마무리하는 것이 가장 적절합니다.

passages.text: The evolution of Western music was profoundly shaped by the development of a standardized system of musical notation during the Middle Ages. Before notation, music was transmitted orally, a process prone to error and change over generations. ( ① ) A piece of music could exist in countless variations, and complex compositions were difficult to preserve and disseminate accurately. ( ② ) The invention of a staff-based system, largely credited to Guido of Arezzo in the 11th century, was a revolutionary step. ( ③ ) It allowed for the precise recording of pitch, and later, rhythm. ( ④ ) Furthermore, the development of notation allowed composers to write down complex polyphonic music, where multiple independent melodic lines are interwoven. ( ⑤ )

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 기보법 발전의 궁극적인 역사적 의의를 설명합니다. (④)까지 기보법의 구체적인 기능과 결과를 설명한 후, 글의 마지막인 (⑤)에 위치하여 전체 내용을 종합하고 그 중요성을 강조하는 것이 가장 논리적입니다.

passages.text: Cooperative learning, where students work together in small groups, is often praised as a powerful educational strategy. However, its effectiveness is not automatic. ( ① ) For cooperative learning to be truly successful, several key conditions must be met. ( ② ) First, there must be "positive interdependence," a sense that the group sinks or swims together. ( ③ ) Each member's effort is required for the group's success. ( ④ ) Second, "individual accountability" is crucial; every student must be responsible for their own learning and for contributing to the group. ( ⑤ ) Finally, clear goals and well-defined roles within the group help to structure the interaction and prevent conflict.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 '개별 책무성'이 없을 때 발생하는 문제점('무임승차자')을 설명합니다. 따라서 '개별 책무성'을 소개한 (④) 다음 문장인 (⑤)에 들어가 그 개념을 부연 설명하는 것이 가장 자연스럽습니다.

passages.text: In the digital age, our personal information is more accessible and persistent than ever before. Every search, purchase, and social media post contributes to a vast digital footprint that can be difficult to manage or erase. ( ① ) This permanence raises significant privacy concerns, as past mistakes or irrelevant information can haunt individuals indefinitely. ( ② ) In response to this problem, the concept of the "right to be forgotten" has emerged, particularly in European law. ( ③ ) The aim is not to rewrite history but to allow people to move on from past events that are no longer relevant, ensuring that their digital identity does not perpetually define their present and future. ( ④ ) This right represents a crucial step in rebalancing the power between individuals and data giants. ( ⑤ )

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 '잊힐 권리'의 구체적인 내용을 설명합니다. (②)에서 '잊힐 권리'라는 개념을 소개한 후, 그 개념이 무엇인지 구체적으로 설명하는 (③)에 들어가는 것이 가장 적절합니다.

passages.text: Surrealism, an influential art movement of the early 20th century, sought to unlock the power of the unconscious mind. ( ① ) Deeply influenced by Sigmund Freud's theories on dreams and the subconscious, Surrealist artists aimed to bypass rational thought and tap into a deeper, more authentic reality. ( ② ) They used techniques like "automatism," which involved automatic drawing or writing without conscious control, to reveal hidden thoughts and desires. ( ③ ) Painters like Salvador Dalí created bizarre, dreamlike landscapes where everyday objects were distorted and placed in unexpected contexts. ( ④ ) This exploration of the irrational was a radical departure from traditional art, which had long prioritized reason and order. ( ⑤ )

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 초현실주의의 '목표'를 설명합니다. (③)에서 구체적인 작품 경향을 설명한 후, 그 경향이 어떤 목표를 가지고 있었는지 설명하는 (④)에 들어가는 것이 논리적입니다.

passages.text: The Ship of Theseus is a thought experiment that raises the question of whether an object that has had all of its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object. ( ① ) The paradox is most notably recorded by the ancient Greek historian Plutarch. ( ② ) In his account, the ship that the hero Theseus sailed on was preserved for a long time by the Athenians. ( ③ ) He documented this gradual replacement of the ship's planks over time, noting that the ship was repaired bit by bit until no original timber remained. ( ④ ) The core philosophical question then arises: is the restored ship the same as the original one? ( ⑤ ) It forces us to consider what constitutes identity—is it the physical material, the form, or something else entirely?

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 (④)에서 제기된 철학적 질문('This puzzle')이 오랫동안 논의되어 왔음을 설명합니다. 따라서 질문이 제기된 직후인 (⑤)에 위치하여 그 질문의 역사적 중요성을 부연하는 것이 자연스럽습니다.

passages.text: The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis, also known as linguistic relativity, is a concept in linguistics and cognitive science. ( ① ) It posits that there is a systematic relationship between the grammatical categories of the language a person speaks and how that person both understands the world and behaves in it. ( ② ) This principle suggests that the structure of a language affects its speakers' worldview or cognition. ( ③ ) For example, some languages have many different words for snow, which might allow their speakers to perceive and think about snow in ways that speakers of English cannot. ( ④ ) The stronger version of the hypothesis, linguistic determinism, claims that language entirely determines thought. ( ⑤ ) Most contemporary linguists support a weaker version, which allows for the influence of language on thought without claiming it is completely determined.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 '강한' 버전에 대한 비판을 나타냅니다. (④)에서 강한 버전을 소개한 후, 그에 대한 비판으로 (⑤)에 들어가고, 그 뒤에 대안인 '약한' 버전을 설명하는 흐름이 자연스럽습니다.

passages.text: The "broken windows" theory is a criminological theory that suggests visible signs of crime, anti-social behavior, and civil disorder create an urban environment that encourages further crime and disorder, including serious crimes. ( ① ) The theory was introduced in 1982 by social scientists James Q. Wilson and George L. Kelling. ( ② ) It states that maintaining and monitoring urban environments in a well-ordered condition may stop further vandalism and escalation into more serious crime. ( ③ ) For instance, a single broken window, if left unrepaired, invites more windows to be broken. ( ④ ) They argue that this sends a signal to potential criminals that the community is disordered and that no one is in control. ( ⑤ ) Therefore, proponents of this theory advocate for a police strategy that targets minor offenses to prevent major ones.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 (④)에서 설명된 '무질서하다는 신호'가 어떤 결과(무정부 상태 분위기, 심각한 범죄 유인)로 이어지는지를 설명합니다. 따라서 신호에 대한 설명 뒤인 (⑤)에 위치해야 합니다.

passages.text: Chiaroscuro is a term in art for the use of strong contrasts between light and dark, usually bold contrasts affecting a whole composition. ( ① ) It is a technical term used by artists and art historians for the use of contrasts of light to achieve a sense of volume in modeling three-dimensional objects and figures. ( ② ) While it became a prominent feature of Renaissance art, its origins can be traced back to ancient Greek and Roman painting. ( ③ ) Artists like Leonardo da Vinci and Caravaggio are renowned for their masterful use of chiaroscuro. ( ④ ) By illuminating figures against a dark background, they could model their subjects with a new level of realism and emotional intensity. ( ⑤ ) This technique was used to create a dramatic sense of volume and three-dimensionality on a flat surface.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 명암대조법의 역사적 의의(중세 양식 탈피)를 설명합니다. (⑤) 앞 문장에서 이 기법의 기술적 효과(입체감)를 설명했으므로, 그 기술적 효과가 어떤 역사적 변화를 이끌었는지 설명하는 주어진 문장이 마지막에 오는 것이 가장 적절합니다.

passages.text: The tragedy of the commons is an economic and social dilemma described by ecologist Garrett Hardin in 1968. ( ① ) It refers to a situation where a shared, limited resource becomes depleted due to individuals acting in their own self-interest. ( ② ) Hardin used the example of a pasture open to all local herders. ( ③ ) Each herder is motivated to add more cattle to their herd, as they receive the full benefit from their own animals. ( ④ ) However, the cost of overgrazing is shared by all herders, leading to the eventual destruction of the pasture. ( ⑤ ) This concept is now widely used to explain problems like overfishing, deforestation, and air pollution.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 하딘의 핵심 주장을 요약합니다. (①)에서 공유지의 비극을 정의한 후, 그 이론을 제창한 하딘의 주장을 (②)에서 소개하고, 그 뒤에 구체적인 예시를 드는 것이 자연스러운 흐름입니다.

passages.text: Moore's Law is the observation made by Intel co-founder Gordon Moore in 1965. ( ① ) He noted that the number of transistors on a microchip doubles approximately every two years, though the cost of computers is halved. ( ② ) This observation is not a physical or natural law but rather a projection of a historical trend. ( ③ ) For decades, the semiconductor industry has used it as a guide for long-term planning and to set targets for research and development. ( ④ ) It has been a primary driver of the digital revolution, enabling the creation of more powerful and affordable electronic devices. ( ⑤ ) While some experts believe its pace is slowing, the principle of exponential growth it represents continues to inspire innovation.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 (④)에서 설명된 '더 강력하고 저렴한 전자기기의 등장'에 대한 구체적인 예시입니다. 따라서 일반적인 진술 뒤에 예시가 오는 (⑤)가 가장 적절합니다.

passages.text: A trophic cascade is an ecological phenomenon triggered by the addition or removal of top predators in a food web. ( ① ) These changes in the predator population result in reciprocal changes in the relative populations of predator and prey through a food chain. ( ② ) They can have dramatic and unforeseen consequences on the ecosystem's structure and function. ( ③ ) A classic example occurred in Yellowstone National Park after wolves were reintroduced in 1995. ( ④ ) The wolves preyed on elk, causing a decrease in the elk population. ( ⑤ ) With fewer elk grazing, willow and aspen trees began to recover, which in turn provided better habitats for beavers and songbirds.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 옐로스톤 사례 전체를 요약하는 결론 문장입니다. 늑대 재도입으로 인한 연쇄 효과를 모두 설명한 후인 (⑤)에 위치하여 그 사례의 의의를 설명하는 것이 가장 논리적입니다.

passages.text: An artificial neural network, the foundational structure of deep learning, learns to perform tasks by processing vast amounts of data. The learning process, often called training, is iterative. ( ① ) During each iteration, the network takes an input, such as an image, and makes a prediction about it. ( ② ) This prediction is then compared to the correct, human-provided label, and the difference between the two is calculated as an "error" or "loss." ( ③ ) This crucial step is known as backpropagation and is the core mechanism by which the network learns. ( ④ ) Essentially, it is a method of assigning responsibility for the error to each neuron and adjusting its contribution. ( ⑤ ) This cycle of prediction, error calculation, and weight adjustment is repeated millions of times until the network's predictions become consistently accurate.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 오차 값을 바탕으로 가중치를 조정하는 역전파의 핵심 과정을 설명합니다. (②)에서 오차를 계산한 후, 그 오차를 바탕으로 행동하는 주어진 문장이 (③)에 오고, (③) 다음 문장이 이 과정을 '역전파'라고 명명하는 것이 자연스럽습니다.

passages.text: The American Revolution was not merely a war for independence; it was also an intellectual revolution deeply rooted in the ideas of the Enlightenment. Thinkers of this era championed concepts like natural rights, liberty, and the social contract. ( ① ) John Locke, an English philosopher, was particularly influential. ( ② ) His theory that governments are instituted to protect the rights to life, liberty, and property provided the colonists with a powerful justification for rebelling against British rule. ( ③ ) Similarly, the writings of Montesquieu, particularly his advocacy for the separation of powers, profoundly shaped the structure of the new American government. ( ④ ) The framers of the U.S. Constitution drew upon this idea to create a system of checks and balances among the legislative, executive, and judicial branches. ( ⑤ ) Thus, the foundational documents of the United States are a testament to the enduring legacy of Enlightenment philosophy.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 로크의 원리가 독립선언서에 반영되었다고 설명합니다. (②)에서 로크의 이론을 설명한 후, 그 이론의 영향을 보여주는 주어진 문장이 (③)에 오고, 그 뒤에 또 다른 사상가(몽테스키외)의 영향으로 넘어가는 것이 자연스럽습니다.

passages.text: In economics, an externality is a cost or benefit caused by a producer that is not financially incurred or received by that producer. A negative externality occurs when the production or consumption of a product results in a cost to a third party. ( ① ) Consider a factory that pollutes a river. ( ② ) The cost of this pollution—such as harm to fish stocks, reduced water quality for downstream communities, and health problems—is borne by society, not by the factory. ( ③ ) The factory's private cost of production includes labor, materials, and energy, but it does not include the cost of the environmental damage it creates. ( ④ ) This discrepancy means the true cost of production to society is not reflected in the market price of the goods. ( ⑤ ) Governments may try to correct this market failure through taxes, regulations, or tradable permits.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 시장 가격에 진정한 비용이 반영되지 않을 때의 결과(과잉 생산)를 설명합니다. (④)에서 가격에 비용이 반영되지 않는다는 문제를 지적한 후, 그 결과로 (⑤)에 들어가는 것이 가장 논리적입니다.

passages.text: The concept of "cultural lag," introduced by sociologist William F. Ogburn, describes a situation where the material culture of a society changes more rapidly than its non-material culture. Material culture includes technology, inventions, and physical objects, while non-material culture encompasses values, beliefs, norms, and social institutions. ( ① ) A classic example of this phenomenon can be seen in the development of the internet and digital communication technologies. ( ② ) These technologies evolved at a breathtaking pace, fundamentally altering how people interact, work, and access information. ( ③ ) However, the non-material culture, such as laws regarding online privacy, ethical guidelines for data usage, and social etiquette for digital interactions, has developed much more slowly. ( ④ ) Consequently, a period of maladjustment arises where established social norms and moral codes struggle to adapt to the new material conditions. ( ⑤ ) Navigating this lag requires conscious effort from individuals and societies to create new frameworks that align with technological realities.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 물질 문화와 비물질 문화 사이의 '격차(gap)'가 초래하는 구체적인 사회 문제들을 나열합니다. (④)에서 이 격차로 인한 '부조정의 시기'를 언급한 후, 그 시기의 구체적인 문제들로 (⑤)에 들어가는 것이 자연스럽습니다.

passages.text: DNA replication is the biological process of producing two identical replicas of DNA from one original DNA molecule. This process is fundamental for cell division and the transmission of genetic information. It begins when an enzyme called helicase unwinds the DNA double helix, separating it into two single strands. ( ① ) These separated strands then serve as templates for creating new complementary strands. ( ② ) An enzyme named DNA polymerase moves along each template strand, adding matching nucleotides to build the new DNA molecule. ( ③ ) However, because DNA polymerase can only synthesize DNA in one direction, one of the new strands is made continuously, while the other is synthesized in small, discontinuous pieces. ( ④ ) Another enzyme, DNA ligase, then joins these small pieces, known as Okazaki fragments, into a single, unified strand. ( ⑤ ) The result is two new DNA molecules, each consisting of one original and one newly synthesized strand.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 (③)에서 설명된 연속적 가닥과 불연속적 가닥에 각각 '선도 가닥'과 '지연 가닥'이라는 명칭을 부여합니다. 따라서 두 종류의 가닥이 생성되는 이유를 설명한 직후인 (④)에 위치해야 합니다.

passages.text: Impressionism, which emerged in the late 19th century, marked a radical departure from traditional academic painting. Artists like Claude Monet and Pierre-Auguste Renoir focused on capturing the fleeting effects of light and atmosphere. ( ① ) They often painted outdoors, using rapid, visible brushstrokes to convey the immediate sensation of a particular moment. ( ② ) The subject matter was typically modern life, landscapes, and scenes of leisure, rendered without idealized perfection. ( ③ ) However, by the 1880s, a new generation of artists began to feel that the Impressionist focus on optical reality was too restrictive. ( ④ ) They sought instead to imbue their work with more solid, durable structure and a greater degree of personal, emotional expression. ( ⑤ ) While these artists built upon the Impressionists' use of vivid color, they each developed a unique style that paved the way for modern art.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 (④)에서 설명된 예술적 목표의 변화가 '후기 인상주의'라는 새로운 운동을 낳았다고 설명합니다. 따라서 목표 변화에 대한 설명 뒤, 그리고 후기 인상주의자들의 공통점을 설명하는 문장 앞인 (⑤)가 가장 적절합니다.

passages.text: Biomagnification, also known as bioamplification, is the process by which the concentration of a persistent toxin increases in the tissues of organisms at successively higher levels in a food chain. This phenomenon occurs because the toxin is absorbed or ingested by an organism and is not easily broken down or excreted. ( ① ) When this organism is eaten by another, the toxin is transferred to the predator. ( ② ) Because the predator consumes many contaminated prey over its lifetime, the toxin accumulates to a much higher concentration in its body. ( ③ ) This process repeats at each trophic level. ( ④ ) For instance, a top predator like an eagle might consume fish that have eaten smaller organisms, accumulating toxins from every level of the food web below it. ( ⑤ ) This can lead to severe health issues, reproductive failure, and even death in these apex species.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 생물 농축 과정의 최종 결과를 요약합니다. (④)에서 최상위 포식자의 구체적인 예시를 든 후, 그 일반적인 결과를 설명하는 주어진 문장이 (⑤)에 오고, 그 뒤에 건강에 미치는 영향으로 이어지는 것이 자연스럽습니다.

passages.text: The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis posits a compelling relationship between the language a person speaks and how that person understands the world. It exists in two forms: a "strong" version and a "weak" version. ( ① ) The strong version, known as linguistic determinism, argues that language dictates thought and that linguistic categories limit and determine cognitive categories. ( ② ) According to this view, our reality is fundamentally shaped by the grammatical and lexical structures of our native tongue. ( ③ ) This does not mean, however, that speakers of a language lacking a specific word for a color are incapable of perceiving that color. ( ④ ) Rather, it suggests they may find it more difficult to categorize or remember it, highlighting a subtle influence rather than an insurmountable barrier. ( ⑤ ) Most contemporary linguists and cognitive scientists support this weaker form of the hypothesis.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 언어 상대성, 즉 '약한' 버전의 가설을 정의합니다. (④)에서 약한 버전을 소개한 후, 그에 대한 정의로 (⑤)에 들어가는 것이 가장 논리적입니다.

passages.text: The second law of thermodynamics is a fundamental principle of physics with profound implications. It is often stated in terms of entropy, which can be understood as a measure of disorder or randomness in a system. ( ① ) The law asserts that the total entropy of an isolated system can only increase over time; it can never decrease. ( ② ) An isolated system is defined as one that cannot exchange either energy or matter with its surroundings. ( ③ ) This principle explains why certain processes occur spontaneously while others do not. ( ④ ) For example, heat naturally flows from a hot object to a cold one, increasing the overall disorder, but the reverse never happens on its own. ( ⑤ ) In the real world, truly isolated systems are rare, but the concept is crucial for theoretical understanding.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 고립계 개념의 가장 궁극적인 예시로 우주를 들고 있습니다. (⑤) 앞 문장에서 실제 세계에서는 고립계가 드물지만 이론적으로 중요하다고 한 후, 그 가장 큰 이론적 예시로 우주를 언급하는 것이 자연스럽습니다.

passages.text: Cognitive dissonance is a psychological theory that refers to the mental stress experienced by a person who holds two or more contradictory beliefs, ideas, or values, or performs an action that is contradictory to one of these. ( ① ) This state of internal inconsistency causes a feeling of discomfort. ( ② ) To alleviate this mental discomfort, the individual is motivated to change one of these elements to restore balance. ( ③ ) For example, a person who smokes cigarettes (behavior) while knowing that smoking causes cancer (cognition) will experience cognitive dissonance. ( ④ ) They might decide to quit smoking, dismiss the evidence about cancer, or rationalize their behavior by saying they only smoke socially. ( ⑤ ) The theory, proposed by Leon Festinger, suggests that people have a fundamental drive to seek consistency among their cognitions.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 인지 부조화 이론의 핵심, 즉 불일치가 있을 때 그것을 제거하기 위해 무언가가 변해야 한다는 점을 요약합니다. (⑤) 앞 문장에서 구체적인 해결책 예시를 든 후, 그 근본적인 원리를 설명하는 주어진 문장이 마지막에 오는 것이 자연스럽습니다.

passages.text: The Dunning-Kruger effect is a cognitive bias in which people with low ability in a specific area tend to overestimate their competence. ( ① ) This paradox occurs because incompetent individuals lack the metacognitive ability to realize how poorly they are performing. ( ② ) For instance, a novice musician might believe their performance was flawless, unable to perceive the subtle errors in timing and pitch that an expert would immediately notice. ( ③ ) Conversely, the effect also describes a related bias where highly competent individuals may underestimate their relative ability. ( ④ ) They might mistakenly assume that tasks which are easy for them are also easy for others, leading them to misjudge their own level of expertise. ( ⑤ ) This dual-faceted bias highlights the complex relationship between self-perception and actual ability.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 더닝-크루거 효과의 역설이 발생하는 이유를 설명합니다. (①) 앞 문장에서 역설을 언급한 후, 그 이유를 설명하는 주어진 문장이 오고, 그 뒤에 메타인지 능력 부족이라는 구체적인 설명이 이어지는 것이 자연스럽습니다.

passages.text: Lev Vygotsky's sociocultural theory of learning emphasizes the fundamental role of social interaction in the development of cognition. He believed that learning is a deeply social process. ( ① ) A key concept in his theory is the "Zone of Proximal Development" (ZPD), which is the gap between what a learner can do alone and what they can achieve with guidance. ( ② ) Vygotsky argued that a child first learns new skills and concepts on a social level, through interaction with a more knowledgeable other, such as a parent or teacher. ( ③ ) Then, the child gradually internalizes this learning, transforming it into an individual, internal cognitive process. ( ④ ) For example, a child first learns to solve a puzzle with a parent's help, and eventually, they can solve similar puzzles on their own by using the strategies they learned. ( ⑤ )

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 '내면화 과정'의 중요성을 설명합니다. (③)에서 내면화 과정을 소개한 후, 그 과정의 중요성을 설명하는 주어진 문장이 (④)에 오고, 그 뒤에 구체적인 예시가 이어지는 것이 논리적입니다.

passages.text: The philosophy of marketing has undergone a significant evolution over the past century. In the early 20th century, many businesses operated under a production orientation, assuming that consumers would favor products that were available and highly affordable. ( ① ) The focus was on achieving high production efficiency and wide distribution. ( ② ) Later, a sales orientation became dominant, where companies focused on aggressive selling and promotion to overcome consumer resistance. ( ③ ) However, in the latter half of the 20th century, a new approach emerged: the marketing concept. ( ④ ) It holds that achieving organizational goals depends on knowing the needs and wants of target markets and delivering the desired satisfactions better than competitors do. ( ⑤ ) Instead of "make and sell," the philosophy became "sense and respond."

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.

passages.text: Quantum mechanics, the physics of the very small, introduced concepts that radically challenged our classical, everyday intuition about the world. passages.text: Bioaccumulation is the process by which certain toxic substances accumulate in the tissues of a living organism over time. ( ① ) This occurs when an organism absorbs a substance at a rate faster than that at which the substance is lost by catabolism and excretion. ( ② ) As a result, the concentration of the toxin within the organism can become much higher than its concentration in the surrounding environment. ( ③ ) For example, small fish might absorb low levels of mercury from the water. ( ④ ) When a larger fish eats many of these smaller fish, the mercury from each of them is transferred and accumulates in the larger fish's body. ( ⑤ ) This process is then magnified at each successive level of the food chain, a phenomenon known as biomagnification.

passages.text: The human brain has a natural tendency to categorize information to make sense of a complex world. ( ① ) For instance, we quickly learn to categorize animals into groups like "mammals," "birds," and "insects," which helps us make rapid judgments and predictions about them. ( ② ) This ability to generalize is a fundamental aspect of learning and survival. ( ③ ) A stereotype is essentially an oversimplified and often inaccurate generalization about a group of people. ( ④ ) Once formed, these stereotypes can be highly resistant to change due to confirmation bias, where we selectively notice information that supports our existing beliefs. ( ⑤ ) This can lead to prejudice and discrimination, demonstrating how a useful mental tool can have damaging social consequences.

passages.text: In logic and critical thinking, there are two primary methods of reasoning: deduction and induction. Deductive reasoning starts with a general statement or hypothesis and works its way down to a specific conclusion. ( ① ) If the initial premises are true, then the conclusion is guaranteed to be true. ( ② ) For example, if the premise is "All birds have feathers" and "A robin is a bird," the deductive conclusion is "A robin has feathers." ( ③ ) For instance, if you observe that every swan you have ever seen is white, you might induce the general rule that "all swans are white." ( ④ ) This type of reasoning allows for the conclusion to be false even if the premises are true, as it is based on probability rather than certainty. ( ⑤ ) Both forms of reasoning are crucial tools in scientific inquiry and everyday problem-solving.

passages.text: ( ① ) In our daily lives, we observe that things tend to become messy over time. A clean room, if left alone, will accumulate dust and clutter. passages.text: The theory of comparative advantage, developed by economist David Ricardo, is a cornerstone of international trade theory. ( ② ) For instance, imagine Country A can produce both wine and cloth more efficiently than Country B. ( ③ ) However, if Country A is vastly more efficient at producing wine than cloth, it should focus its resources on wine production. ( ④ ) Country B, in turn, should specialize in cloth, even if it is less efficient in absolute terms. By trading with each other, both nations can consume more of both goods than they could in isolation. ( ⑤ )

passages.text: Determinism is the philosophical view that all events, including human actions, are ultimately determined by causes external to the will. ( ② ) A determinist would argue that if one knew all the physical laws of the universe and the initial conditions, one could, in principle, predict the entire future. ( ③ ) This view holds that despite external influences and internal dispositions, humans are the ultimate authors of their own actions. ( ④ ) The debate between these two opposing views has been a central and enduring problem in philosophy. ( ⑤ ) Some philosophers, known as compatibilists, have attempted to reconcile the two, arguing that free will and determinism can coexist.

passages.text: The placebo effect is a fascinating phenomenon where a patient experiences real health improvements from a treatment with no actual therapeutic value. passages.text: ( ① ) Before Keynes, classical economics held that economies would naturally return to full employment without government intervention. passages.text: Stoicism, an ancient school of philosophy founded in Athens, offers a practical guide to living a virtuous life. ( ② ) The Stoics taught that we cannot control external events, but we can control our judgments about them. ( ③ ) Therefore, the path to happiness lies in focusing on what is within our power—our thoughts, attitudes, and actions. ( ④ ) By distinguishing between what we can and cannot control, we can avoid being disturbed by misfortune or the actions of others. ( ⑤ )

passages.text: Confirmation bias is the cognitive tendency to search for, interpret, favor, and recall information in a way that confirms or supports one's preexisting beliefs. passages.text: The world of marketing can be broadly divided into two distinct categories: traditional and digital. ( ② ) The primary characteristic of this approach is its wide, and often untargeted, reach, casting a broad net to capture as many potential customers as possible. ( ④ ) For instance, social media marketing and search engine optimization (SEO) are powerful tools within this domain. ( ⑤ ) While both approaches aim to attract customers, they operate on fundamentally different principles and utilize different channels.

passages.text: Democracy, a system of government by the whole population, can be implemented in different ways. The two primary forms are direct democracy and representative democracy. ( ② ) While it represents the purest form of popular rule, it can be cumbersome and inefficient, especially as the population grows. ( ③ ) On the other hand, representative democracy is often considered more practical for large, modern nations where such direct participation is unfeasible. ( ④ ) These elected officials are accountable to the people and can be replaced in subsequent elections. ( ⑤ ) Thus, the choice between these systems often involves a trade-off between direct citizen control and governmental efficiency.

passages.text: The transition from the Classical to the Romantic era in Western music represents a significant shift in artistic philosophy. ( ① ) Composers like Haydn and Mozart are prime examples, creating works that are elegant, restrained, and formally precise. ( ② ) Their music often follows predictable patterns and structures, such as the sonata form, aiming for universal beauty rather than personal confession. ( ③ ) Conversely, the Romantic era that followed prioritized emotional expression and individual subjectivity above all else. ( ④ ) Composers such as Beethoven, Schubert, and Chopin broke free from the rigid constraints of the earlier period, exploring new harmonies, dynamic ranges, and longer, more complex forms. ( ⑤ ) This fundamental change in focus redefined the purpose and potential of music for generations to come.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 낭만주의 음악의 특징을 설명합니다. (③)에서 낭만주의 시대를 소개한 후, 그 시대 음악의 특징을 설명하는 주어진 문장이 (④)에 오는 것이 가장 적절합니다.

passages.text: Virtual Reality (VR) and Augmented Reality (AR) are two transformative technologies that are often confused, but they offer fundamentally different experiences. VR aims to create a completely immersive, artificial world that replaces the user's real-world surroundings. ( ① ) Users typically wear a headset that blocks out external stimuli, transporting them to a computer-generated environment, be it a fantasy landscape or a simulated training ground. ( ② ) This total immersion is the defining feature of the VR experience. ( ③ ) Augmented Reality (AR), however, operates on a different principle by enhancing the user's existing environment rather than replacing it. ( ④ ) A well-known example is the mobile game Pokémon GO, where players see digital creatures in their actual physical surroundings through their phone's camera. ( ⑤ ) In essence, VR creates a new reality, while AR alters the one we already perceive.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 증강현실(AR)이 어떻게 작동하는지를 구체적으로 설명합니다. (③)에서 AR을 소개한 후, 그 작동 원리를 설명하는 주어진 문장이 (④)에 오는 것이 가장 논리적입니다.

passages.text: Symbiosis describes a close and long-term interaction between two different biological species. These relationships can be categorized based on the outcome for each participant. ( ① ) One of the most well-known types is mutualism, in which both species benefit from the interaction. ( ② ) For example, bees get nectar from flowers for food, and in the process, they pollinate the flowers, allowing them to reproduce. ( ③ ) A starkly different form of symbiosis is parasitism, where the relationship is not mutually beneficial. ( ④ ) A classic example is a tick feeding on the blood of a dog; the tick gains nourishment while the dog suffers from blood loss and potential disease transmission. ( ⑤ ) Understanding these different symbiotic relationships is crucial for comprehending the complex web of life in any ecosystem.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 기생 관계의 정의를 설명합니다. (③)에서 기생 관계를 소개한 후, 그 정의를 설명하는 주어진 문장이 (④)에 오고, 그 뒤에 구체적인 예시가 이어지는 것이 자연스럽습니다.

passages.text: In academic and scientific fields, research methodologies are often categorized as either quantitative or qualitative. Quantitative research is concerned with collecting and analyzing numerical data to identify patterns, make predictions, and test causal relationships. ( ① ) It typically involves large sample sizes and statistical analysis to ensure that the findings are generalizable to a larger population. ( ② ) The goal is often to quantify a problem and understand its prevalence by seeking objective, measurable evidence. ( ③ ) Qualitative research, in contrast, focuses on understanding the 'why' and 'how' through non-numerical data like interviews and observations. ( ④ ) Instead of large samples, it often uses smaller, more focused samples to explore topics in rich detail. ( ⑤ ) Both methods have their own strengths and are often used in combination to provide a more comprehensive understanding of a research problem.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 질적 연구의 목표를 설명합니다. (③)에서 질적 연구를 소개한 후, 그 목표를 설명하는 주어진 문장이 (④)에 오는 것이 가장 적절합니다.

passages.text: The concept of a mindset, popularized by psychologist Carol Dweck, profoundly influences how we approach challenges and learning. A person with a fixed mindset believes that their basic qualities, like intelligence or talent, are simply fixed traits. ( ① ) They assume they have a certain amount of ability and that's that, which leads them to avoid challenges for fear of appearing less capable. ( ② ) When faced with a setback, they may easily become discouraged or defensive, viewing failure as a definitive statement of their limitations. ( ③ ) Conversely, individuals with a growth mindset believe that their talents can be developed through hard work, good strategies, and input from others. ( ④ ) They see failure not as a lack of ability but as a valuable opportunity to grow and improve their existing skills. ( ⑤ ) Therefore, cultivating a growth mindset is a key factor in achieving personal and professional success.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 '성장형 사고방식'이 가져오는 긍정적인 결과를 설명합니다. (③)에서 성장형 사고방식을 소개한 후, 그 믿음이 어떤 결과를 낳는지 설명하는 주어진 문장이 (④)에 오는 것이 자연스럽습니다.

passages.text: Energy sources are fundamentally classified into two types: non-renewable and renewable. Non-renewable energy comes from sources that will run out or will not be replenished in our lifetimes—or even in many, many lifetimes. ( ① ) Most non-renewable energy sources are fossil fuels, such as coal, petroleum, and natural gas. ( ② ) The burning of these fuels is a primary contributor to air pollution and climate change, posing significant environmental risks. ( ③ ) Renewable energy sources, on the other hand, are replenished naturally and are considered environmentally sustainable. ( ④ ) For example, solar panels convert sunlight into electricity without emitting harmful greenhouse gases. ( ⑤ ) The transition from non-renewable to renewable energy is one of the most critical challenges of the 21st century.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 재생 가능 에너지원이 어디서 힘을 얻는지를 구체적으로 설명합니다. (③)에서 재생 가능 에너지원을 소개한 후, 그에 대한 구체적인 설명으로 (④)에 들어가는 것이 가장 논리적입니다.

passages.text: Among the Hellenistic philosophies, Stoicism and Epicureanism both offered frameworks for achieving a good life, though their approaches differed significantly. Stoicism taught that virtue, the highest good, is based on knowledge and that the wise live in harmony with divine Reason that governs nature. ( ① ) The Stoics aimed for a state of *ataraxia*, or freedom from passion, by accepting events as they come without emotional disturbance. ( ② ) This state of tranquility was the ultimate goal for its followers. ( ③ ) Similarly, Epicureanism also sought a tranquil life, though it defined the path to it through the pursuit of modest pleasures and the avoidance of pain. ( ④ ) While a Stoic finds peace through rational acceptance of fate, an Epicurean finds it by carefully managing desires. ( ⑤ ) Despite their differences, both philosophies provided enduring guidance on how to live a meaningful and untroubled life.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 에피쿠로스주의가 추구하는 '겸손한 쾌락'이 무엇인지 구체적으로 설명합니다. (③)에서 에피쿠로스주의를 소개한 후, 그 구체적인 내용으로 (④)에 들어가는 것이 가장 자연스럽습니다.

passages.text: Speciation, the process by which new biological species arise, can occur through different mechanisms. One major mode is allopatric speciation, which involves a geographic barrier. ( ① ) This happens when a population is divided by a physical obstacle, such as a mountain range, a river, or a glacier, preventing gene flow between the separated groups. ( ② ) Over time, these isolated populations evolve independently, accumulating genetic differences due to mutation, natural selection, and genetic drift, until they can no longer interbreed. ( ③ ) In contrast, sympatric speciation occurs when new species evolve from a single ancestral species while inhabiting the same geographic region. ( ④ ) In this scenario, there is no physical barrier separating the populations. ( ⑤ ) Both mechanisms demonstrate how the diversity of life on Earth can emerge from a common ancestry.

questions.text: 글의 흐름으로 보아, 주어진 문장이 들어가기에 가장 적절한 곳을 고르시오.
questions.explanation: 주어진 문장은 동소적 종분화에서 생식적 격리가 어떻게 발생하는지를 설명합니다. (④)에서 물리적 장벽이 없다고 한 후, 그 대신 어떤 요인들이 작용하는지 설명하는 주어진 문장이 (⑤)에 오는 것이 가장 논리적입니다.
